metadata.summary: Configure Nginx as a production load balancer with upstream keepalive, TLS 1.2+/1.3, rate limiting zones, security headers, WebSocket support, and path-based routing.

content.title: Nginx Production Load Balancer Configuration
content.summary: Production Nginx LB: worker_processes auto with worker_rlimit_nofile 65535, upstream zones for shared state, keepalive connections for HTTP/1.1 reuse, TLS 1.2+/1.3 with ssl_stapling, rate limiting zones (limit_req_zone + limit_conn_zone), security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options), WebSocket upgrade support, and proxy_next_upstream for automatic retry on failure.

why.p: Nginx's strength is combining the web server and load balancer roles: it serves static content from disk, caches upstream responses, and proxies dynamic requests — all in one process with minimal configuration overhead. Its location block model and upstream zone support make path-based routing and shared state across workers trivial. The passive health check model (max_fails + fail_timeout) covers 95% of use cases without requiring Nginx Plus.

list.item: Web server plus LB combo — serve static assets from Nginx while proxying dynamic traffic to backends.
Static content caching — use proxy_cache to cache upstream responses at the LB layer.
Path-based routing — separate /api, /ws, /admin traffic to different upstream pools.
WebSocket proxying — native upgrade header support.
Simpler configuration than HAProxy for HTTP-only workloads.
TCP-heavy environments where 10-15% HAProxy performance advantage matters at scale.
Advanced circuit breaking or fine-grained retry control — use HAProxy or Envoy.
Active health checks without Nginx Plus — open source Nginx only supports passive (max_fails) health checking.

example.title: Nginx basic HTTP load balancing with backup
Full production Nginx: TLS, rate limiting, keepalive, WebSocket, security headers

rule.statement: Upstream keepalive MUST use proxy_http_version 1.1 and proxy_set_header Connection "" to enable connection reuse. Without these, Nginx uses HTTP/1.0 and closes every upstream connection after use.
rule.rationale: HTTP/1.0 does not support keepalive; every request opens a new TCP connection to the backend. At 1000 req/s this creates 1000 new connections per second, exhausting ephemeral ports and overwhelming the backend's accept queue. The keepalive directive in upstream only activates with HTTP/1.1 and empty Connection header.

rule.statement: ssl_prefer_server_ciphers MUST be set to off for TLS 1.3 (where the client chooses the cipher). TLS 1.2 cipher order is still controlled by ssl_ciphers; use Mozilla Configurator intermediate profile, not a custom list.
rule.rationale: Outdated cipher lists (pre-2020) still circulate in copy-paste configs. Mozilla SSL Configurator generates a current, tested intermediate profile. Enabling ssl_prefer_server_ciphers on for TLS 1.3 causes silent negotiation failures with some clients.

checklist.check: Worker processes match CPU cores (worker_processes auto).
Upstream zone configured for shared state across workers.
Keepalive connections enabled (keepalive 32 in upstream block).
proxy_http_version 1.1 and proxy_set_header Connection "" set for keepalive.
max_fails and fail_timeout set for passive health checking.
Gzip compression enabled for text types.

antipattern.description: Setting worker_connections 100000 without verifying ulimit -n and kernel nofile limits.
antipattern.reason: Nginx opens file descriptors for each connection (2 per proxied connection — client and upstream). If the OS nofile limit is lower than worker_processes * worker_connections, Nginx silently drops connections at the limit. Always verify: ulimit -n returns at least 2 * worker_processes * worker_connections.

antipattern.description: Overlapping NGINX location prefixes without explicit precedence control.
antipattern.reason: NGINX evaluates location blocks: exact match (=) then longest prefix match then regex. Agents frequently emit overlapping prefixes like /api and /api/v1 without understanding that /api/v1 wins only because it is longer, not because of declaration order. Use = for exact matches, ^~ to prevent regex matching, or ensure prefixes are disjoint.
